united-states.name: United States
united-states.primer: For weeks the US and Iran have been locked in an escalating standoff centred on the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow sea gate at the mouth of the Persian Gulf that carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil. Peace talks aimed at a 'memorandum of understanding' over the strait collapsed, and the two sides slid into near-daily tit-for-tat strikes. This weekend the confrontation crossed a dangerous line: US troops were killed in action, turning a mostly indirect conflict into direct combat between the two militaries.

stories.title: The US and Iran are now trading direct strikes — three American troops killed, and Washington bombs Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz
stories.moot: A single strategic chokepoint can turn a distant war into a worldwide price shock: when about 20% of the planet's seaborne oil squeezes through one narrow strait, a conflict there becomes everyone's problem.

stories.title: A US judge freezes the $81 billion Paramount–Warner Bros merger — 12 states win a two-week pause on antitrust grounds
stories.moot: A signed corporate deal isn't final until regulators and courts clear it — competition law can freeze or unwind even the biggest mergers if the public is judged likely to lose choice or pay more.

stories.title: 'El Mayo' — the last old-guard boss of Mexico's Sinaloa Cartel — is sentenced to life in a US prison
stories.moot: Jailing a crime boss removes the man but not the market: as long as demand and profits exist, the organisation usually survives and a violent contest for the empty throne can follow.

stories.title: Meta goes on trial in Tennessee over claims Instagram was built to hook kids — the first of several 'addiction' cases
stories.moot: When a product is free, the users' attention is the thing being sold — and courts are increasingly asking whether designing for maximum attention, especially among children, crosses from clever into harmful.

stories.title: Lockheed unveils a cheaper Patriot missile — a scramble to fix air defense's 'cost problem' as drones overwhelm the world's shields
stories.moot: In modern warfare, whoever wins on cost-per-shot can win the war of attrition: spending millions to destroy something that cost thousands is a losing trade, so cheaper defenses can matter as much as more powerful ones.

stories.title: A mass shooting tears through downtown Tucson's nightlife strip — 9 wounded, and a police officer shoots the fleeing suspect
stories.moot: In a country with widespread access to firearms, an ordinary dispute in a crowded public place can escalate into many casualties in seconds — which is why the presence of a gun, more than the original quarrel, often determines how deadly a confrontation becomes.

stories.title: Apple overtakes Nvidia to become the world's most valuable company as the AI trade shifts
stories.moot: Being 'the most valuable company' is a live, wobbly title, not a permanent crown — it flips as investors re-guess where the next wave of profit will come from. In a boom, the safest-looking giant often wins once the frenzy over the flashiest one cools.

stories.title: A flash flood tears through a Utah canyon and kills a firefighter's whole family — at least 8 dead as severe storms batter the US
stories.moot: A warmer atmosphere holds more water and releases it in sharper bursts, so the danger isn't just how much rain falls but how fast — narrow terrain can turn a distant storm into a lethal wall of water in minutes.

stories.title: FBI agents told to stop investigating ICE — the main outside check on immigration-agent violence is being pulled back
stories.moot: Accountability depends on who does the checking: when an agency's conduct is reviewed only by another arm of the same department, the independence that makes oversight meaningful disappears.

stories.title: Spain win the 2026 World Cup — a single extra-time goal ends Messi's last dance
stories.moot: In tournament football, a disciplined, cohesive team that controls the whole game often beats a side built around one superstar — structure can outlast individual brilliance.

stories.title: A cheaper-AI scare hits global tech stocks — IBM suffers its worst day since 1987, Asian markets wobble
stories.moot: A boom that lifts a whole sector still creates winners and losers inside it — when a technology gets cheaper, the companies selling the old, expensive version can be the ones that get hurt.

stories.title: A second person dies in a Legionnaires' disease outbreak on Manhattan's Upper East Side — 72 sickened, but officials say the source is likely gone
stories.moot: Deadly infections don't always come from other people — man-made systems like air-conditioning and plumbing can breed and broadcast disease, which is why routine maintenance and inspection are a genuine public-health tool.

stories.title: China's Moonshot AI moves to cash in on its 'Kimi' moment — planning a Hong Kong listing within six months at a $30bn-plus valuation
stories.moot: A breakthrough product is also a fundraising event: companies rush to sell shares when hype and attention are highest, because a hot moment is when investors will pay the most for a stake.

stories.title: Lebanon's president heads to the White House with a plan to disarm Hezbollah — the first Lebanese leader to visit in nearly 20 years
stories.moot: Winning a war on the battlefield is not the same as ending it — lasting peace needs a political deal that gives each side something it wants (here, disarmament in exchange for withdrawal), and it usually needs an outside power with enough leverage to make both sides move.

stories.title: A storm is spinning up in the Gulf of Mexico — forecasters give it an 80% chance of becoming a tropical cyclone within two days, aimed at Florida
stories.moot: A storm's danger isn't just about wind speed — slow, soggy tropical systems often kill more people through inland flooding than through the wind they get named for.

stories.title: Canada's record wildfires push New York and Washington to 'most polluted' — as air turns hazardous across the US Northeast and Midwest
stories.moot: Pollution ignores borders: a fire season in one country can become a health emergency for tens of millions in another. As the climate warms, 'someone else's wildfire' increasingly becomes everyone's air.

stories.title: Cuba frees dissident artist Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara after 5 years — on condition he leave the country, and he lands in Miami
stories.moot: Governments often manage dissent by exporting it — freeing a prominent critic into exile silences them at home while avoiding any admission that the jailing was unjust.

stories.title: Iran hits Kuwait's water-and-power plants, forcing rationing — the Gulf war spreads to civilian lifelines
stories.moot: In modern wars the most damaging targets aren't always armies — hitting the plants that supply a country's water and power can hurt a whole population fast, so heavy dependence on a few critical facilities is itself a strategic weakness.

stories.title: Tate brothers arrested in Miami as UK seeks their extradition on 59 charges including rape and trafficking
stories.moot: Borders don't erase criminal liability: extradition treaties let charges filed in one country reach a suspect living in another — but proving the case and completing the transfer can take years.

stories.title: The World Cup final is here: Spain vs Argentina, Messi vs Yamal — the planet's biggest match, tonight in New Jersey
stories.moot: Global sporting finals are economic events too: for one night they concentrate the world's attention, and money flows to whoever owns the broadcast rights, the sponsorships and the merchandise — attention is a market.

stories.title: The backlash against AI's power-hungry data centres goes national — protests planned in 125+ US towns
stories.moot: Every digital boom has a physical bill: AI may feel weightless, but it runs on real land, power and water — and when those costs land on local communities, the politics can slow the technology down even as the tech keeps advancing.

stories.title: A stomach-bug outbreak pulls Taylor Farms lettuce off US shelves — and Taco Bell's menu
stories.moot: In a globalised food supply, safety is only as strong as its weakest link — one contaminated farm abroad can trigger recalls and menu changes across an entire country almost overnight.

stories.title: Iraq signs big US energy deals to reroute its oil around the Strait of Hormuz
stories.moot: When a single passage carries a huge share of global supply, buyers and sellers will pay a lot to build backups — because in trade, having an alternative route is a form of insurance against conflict.

stories.title: Trump threatens to make Canada pay for its wildfire smoke — through tariffs — as 'filthy' haze from record fires chokes US cities
stories.moot: Tariffs have become an all-purpose political weapon, reached for far beyond their original job of shaping trade — even aimed at things, like drifting smoke, that they cannot actually tax. The episode also shows how a warming climate turns natural disasters into diplomatic disputes: as cross-border harms like wildfire smoke grow, so do the fights over who is to blame and who should pay.

stories.title: Apple overtakes Nvidia as the world's most valuable company — the first time the AI-chip king has lost the crown in nearly a year, as investors cool on the AI trade
stories.moot: The company at the very top of the market is really a snapshot of the story investors currently believe. In a boom, the crowd rushes to the most obvious winner — the one 'selling the shovels' — but when that story is questioned, money rotates to steadier earners, and the crown can change hands without the new leader doing anything new. Leadership is about the narrative as much as the numbers.

stories.title: China's Moonshot AI unveils Kimi K3 — a giant, cheap open model that rivals America's best and triggers a 'DeepSeek moment' chip selloff
stories.moot: When a capable competitor shows up at a fraction of the price, it can reprice an entire industry overnight — because a lot of a market leader's value rests on the belief that its product is scarce and hard to copy. 'Good enough and much cheaper' is often more disruptive than 'the best', and in a gold rush the companies selling the shovels (here, chipmakers) can fall fastest when people suspect the digging just got cheaper.

stories.title: The US fills all 85,000 H-1B work visas — and for the first time picks winners by salary, not luck, hitting India's junior tech hopefuls hardest
stories.moot: How you design a selection rule quietly decides who wins — switching the H-1B from a coin-flip to a salary-weighted draw changes nothing about the 85,000 cap, yet completely reshapes who gets in, favouring experience and high pay over sheer numbers. When demand hugely exceeds a fixed supply, the allocation method (lottery vs. auction vs. merit) matters as much as the supply itself.

stories.title: US senators push a bill to slap 100% tariffs on India for buying Russian oil — New Delhi says it's 'closely following'
stories.moot: Modern economic pressure increasingly works through third parties: rather than only sanctioning an adversary directly, powers threaten that country's trading partners, turning them into pressure points to isolate the target. It's also a reminder that cheap energy can carry a hidden geopolitical price — a bargain barrel today can become a diplomatic liability tomorrow.

stories.title: A cholesterol pill to replace the needle: the US FDA approves Merck's enlicitide, the first pill that works like the powerful injected cholesterol drugs
stories.moot: How a medicine is delivered can matter as much as how well it works. A drug that must be injected reaches far fewer people than an equally effective pill, because convenience drives whether patients actually keep taking it. Turning a hard-to-take treatment into an easy one is itself a form of medical progress — sometimes a bigger one than the underlying chemistry.

stories.title: The EU orders Google to crack open Android and Search — rival AI assistants get 'Hey Google'-style access and competitors get its search data
stories.moot: In digital markets, the real moat isn't a single product — it's control of the gateway (the operating system, the search box) plus the mountain of data that flows through it. That is exactly why regulators are shifting from breaking up companies to forcing them to share access and data: prying open the gateway can do more for competition than any fine.

stories.title: SpaceX stock falls below its own IPO price for the first time — wiping out its whole rally and stripping Elon Musk of 'trillionaire' status a month after the record listing
stories.moot: A share price is a mood, not a measure of a company's worth — especially when only a tiny slice of the stock trades, so hype and fear both get amplified. 'Paper' fortunes made by a hot IPO can evaporate as fast as they appear, because you don't actually have the money until you sell, and the crowd can change its mind overnight.

stories.title: US widens its war on Iran — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling a tanker, as Iran fires back at American bases in the Gulf
stories.moot: Geographic chokepoints are strategic weapons: whoever controls a narrow passage like Hormuz can throttle a huge share of global trade, so a regional conflict there sends prices and risk rippling worldwide. Energy security and geopolitics are inseparable — a war few people can see up close still shows up in the fuel everyone buys.

stories.title: TSMC posts a record quarter and pledges another $100bn to build chips in America — lifting its US bet to $265bn as the AI boom rages
stories.moot: When the company that manufactures nearly all the world's most advanced chips posts record profits and raises its spending, it's a powerful real-world gauge that the AI boom is still building — the 'picks-and-shovels' supplier often reveals more about a gold rush than the miners do. And where critical factories get built is as much about geopolitics as economics.

stories.title: Uber makes a $14.8bn move to swallow Delivery Hero — a bid to build the world's biggest food-delivery firm outside China
stories.moot: In platform businesses, markets tend to collapse toward one or two dominant players — so after a long, loss-making land grab, the endgame is consolidation: the profitable survivor buys the rest to own the network. And big acquirers now design deals to pass antitrust review, pre-selling the overlapping pieces before regulators even ask.

stories.title: Trump slaps a 25% tariff on most Brazilian goods — and Brazil says the real target is its politics, not its trade
stories.moot: Tariffs are increasingly used as political leverage, not just economic policy — a 'trade' measure can really be about elections, alliances and pressure. And the exemptions reveal the intent: sparing coffee and orange juice shows the aim is to squeeze the other country without hurting your own voters at the checkout.

stories.title: The world just tilted toward Beijing: for the first time in about 20 years of polling, more countries view China favourably than the United States, Pew finds — China leads in 25 of 36 nations
stories.moot: Soft power is a national asset you can spend or squander, and it moves with behaviour: alienate allies and it erodes quietly, letting a rival gain ground without firing a shot — because in global affairs, being trusted is itself a form of leverage.

stories.title: India's most famous central banker lands a US seat: ex-RBI chief Raghuram Rajan is co-leading a task force to overhaul the Federal Reserve
stories.moot: A central bank's balance sheet is a hidden throttle on the world's money supply, and because US interest rates anchor global capital, who sits on the Fed's review panels can matter to a saver in Mumbai almost as much as to one in Chicago.

stories.title: After 23 years, America is leaving Iraq: Washington and Baghdad say every US troop will be gone by September 30
stories.moot: Military footprints are political, not permanent: once the original mission fades — here, defeating ISIS — the justification for staying erodes. But pulling out rarely ends a country's problems; it usually just hands influence to whoever is willing to step into the gap.

stories.title: Toronto's air becomes the world's worst as 100+ Ontario wildfires blanket Canada — and the smoke is now drifting over New York and Washington
stories.moot: A wildfire is a local event, but its smoke is a regional one — pollution travels hundreds of miles on the wind, so a fire in one country can send another country's cities indoors. As the climate warms, 'fire season' increasingly means 'smoke season' even for people who live nowhere near the flames.

stories.title: China's DeepSeek raises money at a $74bn valuation — and its founder quietly becomes the world's richest AI-startup billionaire
stories.moot: Cutting off a rival's tools slows them down, but it also forces them to build their own — restrictions can accelerate the very self-reliance they were meant to prevent.

russia.name: Russia
russia.primer: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine has ground on for more than four years, and in recent weeks both sides have escalated long-range strikes on each other's cities and infrastructure. Just yesterday Ukraine launched its deadliest strike inside Russia in over two years, torching warehouses and an oil depot; this barrage is Russia's answer.

stories.title: A Russian missile hits a merchant ship leaving Odesa — four Indian sailors are killed as the war spreads onto civilian cargo vessels
stories.moot: Modern wars rarely stay inside the borders of the countries fighting them: global supply chains are crewed and financed by outsiders, so a missile in the Black Sea can kill Indian sailors and raise bread prices continents away.

stories.title: Ukraine launches its largest drone attack on Moscow in two years — an oil depot and a big warehouse go up in flames
stories.moot: Cheap drones let a smaller army strike deep into a bigger one's home turf — modern wars are increasingly about who can hit the other side's fuel, factories and morale far from the front line, not just who holds ground.

stories.title: Drone strikes halt a major Black Sea oil pipeline's exports — a new front in the Russia-Ukraine war threatens oil supply
stories.moot: Global oil supply runs through a handful of chokepoints and terminals; damage to even one export node — far from the buyer — can ripple into prices worldwide, showing how concentrated and fragile energy logistics really are.

stories.title: Russia hits Kyiv with one of the biggest missile barrages of the war — 40+ missiles and 120+ drones
stories.moot: In modern warfare, defence is a numbers game: attackers try to overwhelm air defences with mass salvos, so a country's safety depends as much on how many interceptors it can keep in stock as on how good each one is.

stories.title: Ukraine lands its deadliest strike inside Russia in over two years: drones set Wildberries warehouses and a Moscow-region oil depot ablaze, killing at least 8
stories.moot: In modern war, logistics and supply chains are front-line targets: a side that cannot win on the battlefield can still hurt its enemy by hitting the fuel, factories and warehouses that keep the war running. And when dual-use civilian sites are struck, the line between military target and civilian harm blurs — which is exactly what each side then fights over in the information war.

stories.title: US senators push a bill to slap 100% tariffs on India for buying Russian oil — New Delhi says it's 'closely following'
stories.moot: Modern economic pressure increasingly works through third parties: rather than only sanctioning an adversary directly, powers threaten that country's trading partners, turning them into pressure points to isolate the target. It's also a reminder that cheap energy can carry a hidden geopolitical price — a bargain barrel today can become a diplomatic liability tomorrow.

stories.title: Zelensky fires Ukraine's popular 'drone-war' defence minister — and mass protests erupt in Kyiv as parliament installs a new prime minister
stories.moot: Even under wartime rule, a leader's power ultimately rests on public trust — and removing a popular, successful official without explaining why can spark the very instability the move was meant to avoid. Transparency isn't a courtesy in a crisis; it's what keeps authority intact when people can't simply vote you out.

stories.title: Ukraine opens a new front at sea: drone strikes choke Russian shipping in the Sea of Azov
stories.moot: Wars are won on logistics as much as on the front line. Striking the ships, canals and fuel that supply an enemy - often far cheaper than fighting soldiers directly - can squeeze a war economy and shift the balance without a single battlefield gain.

stories.title: A 500% tariff wall: Trump backs a Russia sanctions bill that could punish India for buying Russian oil
stories.moot: There is a threshold where a tariff stops being a tax and becomes a blockade. Modest tariffs shift trade at the margin; a 500% one simply ends it. And 'secondary' penalties show how a great power enforces its foreign policy on others - punishing not its rival, but everyone who trades with its rival.

stories.title: Indian-origin NASA astronaut Anil Menon blasts off on his first mission to the ISS
stories.moot: Big scientific endeavours often outlast politics — nations that clash on the ground still cooperate in orbit, because spaceflight is hard enough that no one can do it entirely alone.

stories.title: NATO summit wraps: €70bn for Ukraine, and Trump lets Kyiv build its own Patriot missiles
stories.moot: Owning the factory beats owning the stockpile — a licence to build your own weapons changes a country's leverage far more than any single shipment, but manufacturing capacity takes years to stand up, so the relief lags the announcement.

stories.title: NATO's Ankara summit pledges €70bn for Ukraine — but much of it is old money, and the US won't pay
stories.moot: Read the fine print on big numbers: a headline aid figure can be mostly old money relabelled, and who actually writes the cheque (here, Europe, not the US) tells you more about real commitment than the total does.

stories.title: NATO summit opens in Ankara — Trump demands the money, Ukraine begs for interceptors after its worst night
stories.moot: Alliances are tested not by their promises but by their invoices — a summit reveals how much collective defence a group will actually pay for, and on the battlefield the side that can still shoot down incoming missiles keeps its bargaining power.

stories.title: Russia answers peace talk with its heaviest strike on Kyiv — and Ukraine is running out of interceptors
stories.moot: Diplomacy tends to ratify the facts on the ground, not replace them — which is why a 'peace call' can be followed by a bigger attack. On the battlefield, the side that can still defend its cities keeps its bargaining power; run out of interceptors and you run out of leverage.

stories.title: Ukraine burns a major oil terminal deep inside Russia as Trump phones Putin to talk peace
stories.moot: In modern war, energy infrastructure is a front line of its own — hitting the pipes and terminals that fund an enemy can matter as much as taking territory, and it ripples straight into global prices.

stories.title: Ukraine strikes a major Russian oil terminal in St Petersburg
stories.moot: Modern wars are fought over cash flow — striking the enemy's oil terminals aims at the money that funds the fighting, not just at territory.

stories.title: China promotes new generals after a purge — as Western worry over its Russia ties grows
stories.moot: In autocracies, 'anti-corruption' campaigns double as loyalty tools — who gets purged and who gets promoted tells you where power is really consolidating.

stories.title: Russia hits Kyiv with its biggest attack of the war
stories.moot: In modern warfare, the side that runs out of cheap defences first is often the one that loses the war of attrition — a $50,000 drone can force the use of a $2 million interceptor, so cost, not just courage, decides how long a country can hold on.

stories.title: Russia's fuel squeeze persists after Ukrainian refinery strikes
stories.moot: Damaged Russian refining tightens global fuel supply and keeps energy a live inflation risk — the very thing that had spooked markets.

stories.title: Ukrainian strikes trigger fuel shortages inside Russia
stories.moot: Russia is one of the world's largest oil producers, and fuel is the backbone of both its economy and its war machine.

stories.title: Hamas picks Khalil al-Hayya as its new leader — its lead ceasefire negotiator now heads the group
stories.moot: When a militant or political group loses its top figure, power tends to flow to whoever controls the group's main source of leverage — here, the negotiations — so a leadership pick quietly signals where the real bargaining chips sit.

stories.title: Oil jumps back above $90 as Iran chokes the Strait of Hormuz
stories.moot: Oil prices react to fear as much as to actual supply cuts — the mere threat to a chokepoint like Hormuz can lift the price the whole world pays, because traders price risk, not just today's barrels.

stories.title: Hundreds of Israelis march on the Gaza border to demand Jewish resettlement — with coalition ministers backing them

stories.title: After 23 years, America is leaving Iraq: Washington and Baghdad say every US troop will be gone by September 30
stories.moot: Military footprints are political, not permanent: once the original mission fades — here, defeating ISIS — the justification for staying erodes. But pulling out rarely ends a country's problems; it usually just hands influence to whoever is willing to step into the gap.

stories.title: The Gulf war goes regional: Iran strikes US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan and claims a hit on the Fifth Fleet's HQ, as Trump threatens to bomb Iran's power plants and bridges 'next week'
stories.moot: Wars escalate along two tracks at once — who gets hit and what gets hit. When a conflict spreads from proxies to a rival's own bases, and from military targets to civilian infrastructure like power and bridges, it is widening on both fronts, which is how a contained fight becomes a regional one. And every step up keeps a fear premium under the oil price, even before a single extra barrel is actually lost.

stories.title: Toll off, blockade on: Trump scraps his 20% Hormuz fee but reinstates a naval blockade of Iran's ships — the chokepoint for a fifth of the world's oil
stories.moot: A war has more than one price lever, and they don't always move together. A blockade tightens supply and raises risk, but a fee dropped or added changes the cost too — so watch every lever, not just the loudest headline. Here, scrapping a toll that would have taxed every ship offset some of the fear the blockade created.

stories.title: War-risk hits the pump: Brent jumps ~10% to a one-month high as the Hormuz crisis flares, while AI chip stocks slide
stories.moot: A supply scare moves prices as much as an actual supply cut. Traders price the risk of lost oil, not just the loss itself — so war-risk in a shipping chokepoint shows up at the pump within weeks, and the same fear that lifts crude often drains the market's most crowded trades.

stories.title: A chemical tanker burns off Oman: the Stolt Magnesium is hit by an external blast near Hormuz as Gulf shipping comes under sustained attack
stories.moot: A war reaches the global economy through the price of moving things, not just the price of the thing itself. When ships get hit, insurance and freight jump long before any barrel is actually lost - so the cost of a conflict can rise even while supply keeps flowing.

stories.title: Yemen's Houthis strike Saudi Arabia's Abha airport with missiles and drones - the war widens onto Saudi soil
stories.moot: Regional wars rarely stay contained - allied and proxy forces use one conflict as cover to open new fronts, so a fight between two states can quickly pull in neighbours and turn a local war into a regional one.

stories.title: The Gulf war hits home: India summons Iran's envoy after an Indian sailor is killed in a tanker attack in the Strait of Hormuz
stories.moot: A country's exposure to a distant war is not only about oil or trade - it is about people. When your citizens do a job the whole world depends on (here, crewing tankers), a conflict on the far side of a sea lane can reach your homes directly, and diplomacy then has to protect lives, not just prices.

stories.title: India imports most of its oil: Hormuz 'flip-flops' whipsaw Brent near a 5% jump, and India's refiners are on edge
stories.moot: Price can move on fear alone. For an import-dependent economy it is not the actual cutoff of oil that does the first damage - it is the risk of one. A costlier barrel widens the import bill, weakens the currency and stokes inflation even while every tanker still sails, because markets price the danger, not just today's supply.

stories.title: Blockade on, toll off: the US naval blockade of Iran's ports goes live in the small hours — but Trump drops his 20% Hormuz 'toll', easing Brent back off its ~$87 high
stories.moot: A war has more than one price. The concrete acts — a blockade, a tanker hit, a death — raise the risk, but a fee dropped or added moves the cost too. When oil eases even as warships close in, look for the second lever: here, scrapping a toll that would have taxed every ship offset the fear of the blockade.

stories.title: US–Iran war grinds into a third weekend — Hormuz 'closed,' the Gulf under fire, and oil up ~4% as markets learn to live with it
stories.moot: Markets react to the marginal surprise, not the raw headline. The first time a chokepoint is threatened, prices leap; by the third weekend of the same threat without an actual supply cut, the same news moves them far less — which is why a 'closed' strait lifted oil only ~4% today, and why the real danger is a genuine cutoff that the calm is no longer pricing.

stories.title: Hormuz reignites — Iran shuts the strait and strikes five Gulf states, the US hits 140 targets, and the truce is dead
stories.moot: A truce made only of words can unravel in a single night — and markets that closed pricing peace can be badly wrong-footed by a weekend of war, because prices only move when trading reopens, not when the news breaks. What actually moves oil is a real threat to barrels, and a declared chokepoint closure is exactly that.

stories.title: Overshadowed by the Iran war, Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza as hospitals go dark
stories.moot: A ceasefire is a label, not a guarantee — what matters is the reality underneath it: who controls the ground and whether basic systems like power and hospitals still function; watch conditions, not just declarations.

stories.title: Hormuz de-escalates — Iran calls its tanker attack 'a mistake' and heads to Oman, as the US wants it said out loud
stories.moot: Wars often end in words before they end in fact — the moment adversaries start negotiating over a chokepoint, the fear premium in prices drains even while warships and threats remain, because what actually moves oil is barrels lost, not tension felt.

stories.title: Nine months into a ceasefire, Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza as hospitals go dark
stories.moot: A ceasefire is a label, not a guarantee — what matters is the reality underneath it: who controls the ground and whether basic systems like power and hospitals still function; watch conditions, not just declarations.

stories.title: The war continues — fresh strikes hit southern Iran and Hormuz shipping halts — but oil settles and the panic fades
stories.moot: Markets price the worst plausible outcome first, then walk it back when the catastrophe doesn't arrive — a war can keep raging while the 'war premium' in prices quietly drains, because what moves oil is lost barrels, not scary headlines.

stories.title: Iran buries Khamenei in Mashhad — six days of mourning end, but the new Supreme Leader stays a ghost
stories.moot: In systems built around one person, an unseen leader is itself information — the absence tells you how threatened a state feels and how uncertain its chain of command is, and rivals and markets keep probing that uncertainty until it resolves.

stories.title: The US strikes Iran and calls the ceasefire 'over' — oil spikes toward $80 as Hormuz shuts
stories.moot: A chokepoint is leverage: when a fifth of the world's oil must squeeze through one strait, an exchange of strikes there can move global prices more than any change in how much is pumped — and a ceasefire is a pause that can be un-paused, so markets must keep pricing the risk it holds.

stories.title: Iran buries Khamenei in Mashhad — and bars its own new Supreme Leader from the funeral
stories.moot: In systems built around one person, hiding the leader is itself information — it tells you how threatened a state feels and how uncertain its chain of command is, and rivals and markets probe that uncertainty the moment it shows.

stories.title: Tankers struck in the Strait of Hormuz — the US–Iran truce cracks and oil jumps
stories.moot: A chokepoint is leverage: when a fifth of the world's oil must squeeze through one narrow strait, a single attack there can move global prices more than a big change in how much is pumped — geography, not just supply, sets the energy price.

stories.title: Iran buries Khamenei tomorrow — the new Supreme Leader is still a ghost
stories.moot: In systems built around one person, a leader's death or disappearance is not just a succession question — it is uncertainty about whether the state can still decide, and rivals and markets probe that uncertainty the moment it appears.

stories.title: Iran readies Khamenei's burial in Mashhad — the new Supreme Leader is still a ghost
stories.moot: In systems built around one person, a leader's death or disappearance is not just a succession question — it is uncertainty about whether the state can still decide, and markets and rivals price that uncertainty immediately.

stories.title: Iran's funeral for Khamenei reaches its vengeance-charged peak — the successor still unseen
stories.moot: In systems built around one person, a leader's death or disappearance is not just a personnel question — it is uncertainty about whether the state can still make decisions, and markets and rivals price that uncertainty at once.

stories.title: Hamas dissolves its Gaza government, handing power to a technocratic committee
stories.moot: In peace deals, the sequence is everything: handing over administration is far easier than handing over weapons, so the governance step can advance while the security core stays frozen — progress and deadlock at the same time.

stories.title: The Strait of Hormuz reopens — but Iran wants to charge a fee, and India has just stood down its gas emergency
stories.moot: The price of oil is set as much by the safety of the ROUTE as by how much oil exists — a threat (or a new fee) at one narrow strait can raise energy costs worldwide before a single barrel is lost.

stories.title: Iran threatens to charge tolls in the Strait of Hormuz — and tankers start turning back
stories.moot: The oil price is set as much by the SAFETY OF THE ROUTE as by how much oil exists. A single narrow strait can move global energy costs even if not a single barrel is actually lost.

stories.title: Iran buries its assassinated Supreme Leader in a week-long show of defiance
stories.moot: Markets care less about who leads a country than about whether the transition is orderly — predictability keeps oil and risk premiums low, while uncertainty at the top of an oil power is priced into the whole world's fuel bill.

stories.title: India walks a tightrope in West Asia
stories.moot: The Middle East is India's lifeline for oil and home to a huge Indian workforce whose remittances (money sent home) are a major source of income.

stories.title: Strait of Hormuz tensions keep energy on edge
stories.moot: Any real disruption at Hormuz spikes oil prices worldwide, reigniting exactly the inflation fear the Fed is trying to calm — and hitting oil-importer India hardest.

stories.title: Iran battles to keep control of the Strait of Hormuz
stories.moot: Any threat to shipping through Hormuz sends oil prices sharply higher on fears that supply could be cut — which is exactly what's fuelling the inflation and central-bank story leading today's brief.

iran.name: Iran
iran.primer: For weeks the US and Iran have been locked in an escalating standoff centred on the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow sea gate at the mouth of the Persian Gulf that carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil. Peace talks aimed at a 'memorandum of understanding' over the strait collapsed, and the two sides slid into near-daily tit-for-tat strikes. This weekend the confrontation crossed a dangerous line: US troops were killed in action, turning a mostly indirect conflict into direct combat between the two militaries.

stories.title: The US and Iran are now trading direct strikes — three American troops killed, and Washington bombs Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz
stories.moot: A single strategic chokepoint can turn a distant war into a worldwide price shock: when about 20% of the planet's seaborne oil squeezes through one narrow strait, a conflict there becomes everyone's problem.

stories.title: Iran hits Kuwait's water-and-power plants, forcing rationing — the Gulf war spreads to civilian lifelines
stories.moot: In modern wars the most damaging targets aren't always armies — hitting the plants that supply a country's water and power can hurt a whole population fast, so heavy dependence on a few critical facilities is itself a strategic weakness.

stories.title: US widens its war on Iran — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling a tanker, as Iran fires back at American bases in the Gulf
stories.moot: Geographic chokepoints are strategic weapons: whoever controls a narrow passage like Hormuz can throttle a huge share of global trade, so a regional conflict there sends prices and risk rippling worldwide. Energy security and geopolitics are inseparable — a war few people can see up close still shows up in the fuel everyone buys.
