india.name: India
india.primer: India's monsoon (June–September) delivers most of the year's rain — vital for farming, but capable of deadly flooding and landslides, especially in the Himalayan north and the low-lying northeast. 'Cloudbursts' — sudden, extremely intense downpours — can turn hill streams into torrents within minutes.

stories.title: India's oil import bill jumps ~60% to nearly $50 billion in three months — even as it buys less crude
stories.moot: An import bill is price times volume, not just volume — so a country can buy less of something and still pay far more, because the price and the currency it pays in matter as much as the quantity.

stories.title: India signals the Indus Waters Treaty 'won't survive in its present form' — hardening its freeze on the water pact with Pakistan
stories.moot: Geography is leverage: the country upstream on a shared river holds a quiet but powerful lever over the country downstream, which is why water can become a tool of statecraft, not just an environmental issue.

stories.title: A Russian missile hits a merchant ship leaving Odesa — four Indian sailors are killed as the war spreads onto civilian cargo vessels
stories.moot: Modern wars rarely stay inside the borders of the countries fighting them: global supply chains are crewed and financed by outsiders, so a missile in the Black Sea can kill Indian sailors and raise bread prices continents away.

stories.title: Paytm's profit jumps 79% as India's payments giant turns the corner
stories.moot: Growing users is easy to celebrate, but a business only becomes durable when it turns steady profits — investors ultimately reward companies that convert scale into actual earnings.

stories.title: IndiGo orders over 1,000 jet engines from CFM — a giant bet on India's booming air travel
stories.moot: Airlines buy engines and planes years before they fly them — a giant order today is really a forecast that demand will keep rising for a decade, and it locks in the suppliers and servicing that make that growth possible.

stories.title: India's core industries grew 5% in June — a five-month high, and the first read on a revamped index
stories.moot: Headline economic numbers are only as good as the basket behind them, which is why governments periodically re-base their indices — a technical tune-up that makes every future reading more trustworthy.

stories.title: HDFC Bank delays its CEO's reappointment as independent directors probe deposit-rate claims
stories.moot: In banking, trust is the product — regulators insist an outside authority (here the RBI) sign off on who leads a bank, so that no boss simply installs themselves.

stories.title: Government tells Parliament it has no plan to scrap the LTCG tax on shares — ending months of investor rumours
stories.moot: Investment tax rules almost never change outside the annual Budget, so mid-year 'they're about to scrap the tax' chatter is usually just noise — a reason to ignore rumours rather than trade on them.

stories.title: India says its capital spending has jumped five-fold to ₹90.87 lakh crore over 2014-26 — the engine behind its growth story
stories.moot: A government can power growth by spending on assets rather than handouts — building a road employs people today and lifts the economy's productivity for decades — but it works only as long as the borrowing behind it stays affordable.

stories.title: West Bengal's new BJP government makes Bengali compulsory for all official work from September 1
stories.moot: Language is rarely just about communication in India — controlling the official tongue is a way governments signal identity and win loyalty, so language policy is often politics by other means.

stories.title: Deadly monsoon floods hammer India's north and northeast — J&K toll climbs to 18 as the Army launches 'Operation Jal Rahat' in Assam
stories.moot: In steep or low-lying terrain it isn't the total rainfall that kills but how fast it arrives — a cloudburst can drop a month's rain in an hour, outpacing any drainage or warning system.

stories.title: A cash-theft scandal at the Ram temple reaches the Supreme Court — investigators report 8 arrests and 70 CCTV catches
stories.moot: The bigger the pool of money an institution handles, the more it needs independent audits and cameras — trust alone doesn't guard cash; systems do.

stories.title: The rupee is inching toward a record low as costlier oil eats into India's finances
stories.moot: For a country that imports most of its energy, its currency and the oil price are tied together — when oil rises, the currency often falls, and the pain lands twice on the import bill.

stories.title: India's 'yo-yo' monsoon — drought, then deluge, then drought — threatens crops and the power grid as a 'super' El Niño builds
stories.moot: For farming and power, the timing of rain matters as much as the total: a monsoon that swings between drought and deluge can do more damage than one that is simply a bit below average, because crops and grids need steady water, not feast-or-famine.

stories.title: Modi opens Parliament by touting 7.7% growth 'despite' the Middle East war — India's economy holds up even as oil bites
stories.moot: A country that imports most of its energy is financially tied to distant wars: a conflict thousands of miles away can raise its fuel prices, weaken its currency and shrink its growth — which is why 'growth despite oil' is a genuine test of economic resilience.

stories.title: India's switch to E20 petrol sparks a driver backlash — clogged injectors, lower mileage and a political row
stories.moot: Big energy transitions rarely come free — a policy that helps the country as a whole (less imported oil) can still land real costs on individuals, and how a government handles those costs decides whether the switch is trusted or resented.

stories.title: Tripura's police chief is found dead in his office — police suspect he shot himself with his service pistol
stories.moot: Even the most powerful officials operate under intense, often invisible pressure — and in a live story like a sudden death, the responsible move is to report what's confirmed and clearly flag what is still only 'suspected' until an inquiry settles it.

stories.title: India's IT giants pull back on fresh graduates — delayed joining dates, quietly withdrawn offers, and a salary-hike rethink
stories.moot: When a new technology can do the easiest tasks in a field, the entry-level rung is the first to shrink — which reshapes who gets hired long before it changes senior roles.

stories.title: Sensex sinks 700 points as HDFC Bank's shrinking margins and pricey oil hit Dalal Street
stories.moot: In an index dominated by a few heavyweight banks, one lender's margin miss can sink the whole market — concentration means a single stock's problem becomes everyone's.

stories.title: 'Cockroach' march to Parliament turns violent — police baton-charge protesters in Delhi
stories.moot: Leaderless, online-organised youth movements are hard for governments to negotiate with or shut down — banning a march can turn a grievance into a bigger confrontation rather than ending it.

stories.title: Reliance beats forecasts with a record quarter — ₹23,001 crore profit on strong refining and Jio, and brokerages turn bullish
stories.moot: A single dominant company can move an entire index — when one stock is heavily weighted in a benchmark, its earnings 'beat' or 'miss' colours the whole market's day, regardless of how other companies did.

stories.title: Parliament's monsoon session opens in chaos — both Houses adjourned as the Opposition storms over exam-paper leaks
stories.moot: A legislature can be paralysed without a single vote — controlling the floor through protest and adjournment is itself a political tool, used by oppositions to force an issue onto the agenda.

stories.title: ICICI Bank's profit rises 16% to ₹14,805 crore — India's second-biggest private bank posts another strong quarter
stories.moot: For a bank, falling 'provisions' can flatter profit: when a lender sets aside less for bad loans, earnings rise — so always check whether growth comes from real business or just a smaller safety buffer.

stories.title: Rohit Sharma shuts down retirement talk — his focus stays on India and the 2027 World Cup
stories.moot: In sport, uncertainty about a star's future can overshadow the games themselves — which is why a clear word from the player and the governing body can matter as much as any scoreboard.

stories.title: After losing the England series, captain Gill sounds the alarm on India's fitness: 'we're missing a trick'
stories.moot: In modern sport, availability is a skill in itself — the most talented squad wins nothing if its best players keep breaking down, so managing bodies is as strategic as picking a team.

stories.title: England beat India by 27 runs at Lord's to take the ODI series 2-1 — Duckett's record 141 trumps Rohit's 138
stories.moot: A standout individual performance doesn't guarantee a team result — Rohit's century and Duckett's record both happened, but the match turned on the 27 runs India's chase fell short by.

stories.title: India Inc's mandatory 'giving' hits a record ₹40,794 crore — the money big companies are legally required to spend on society jumps 17%
stories.moot: A rule that ties charitable spending to a share of profits turns social funding into an automatic, growing stream — but it tends to pool where big companies already sit, so a mandate to give doesn't guarantee the money reaches those who need it most.

stories.title: Yes Bank — the lender that nearly collapsed in 2020 — posts a 34% jump in quarterly profit as its turnaround gathers pace
stories.moot: A bank's health shows up in three numbers together — is it lending more, earning more on those loans, and losing less to defaults? Rising profit alongside a falling bad-loan ratio is the signature of a genuine turnaround.

stories.title: India moves to add four more Supreme Court judges — a bill to fight the country's mountain of pending cases
stories.moot: When an institution is overwhelmed, the fix can be as basic as raising its capacity — but even a simple 'add more judges' move can reopen deeper fights, here over the age-old question of who gets to choose them.

stories.title: 20 Trinamool MPs break from Mamata Banerjee to merge with a tiny party — a legal workaround to defect without losing their seats
stories.moot: Laws written to stop a behaviour often leave a legal 'side door' — India's anti-defection law bans individual party-switching, but a coordinated two-thirds 'merger' walks right through the loophole, showing how rules shape the form politics takes rather than always preventing it.

stories.title: India and the EU say their long-delayed free-trade deal is nearly done — aiming to sign by year-end and switch it on in early 2027
stories.moot: Trade deals take years because the last 5% is the hardest — farm goods, carbon rules and rules of origin are where national interests clash most sharply, long after the easy parts are agreed.

stories.title: Madhya Pradesh clears a Uniform Civil Code draft that makes registering a live-in relationship compulsory — skip it, and you could face jail
stories.moot: The same reform can expand freedom and shrink it at once — here, equal inheritance for all children sits beside jail time for not registering a relationship. 'Reform' always has a trade-off in who it empowers and who it polices.

stories.title: Chennamma, wife of former PM H.D. Deve Gowda and matriarch of a political dynasty, dies at 85 in Bengaluru
stories.moot: Behind India's political dynasties are family networks whose personal milestones — births, marriages, deaths — ripple into public life, because kinship and politics are tightly woven in the country's regional parties.

stories.title: Exam-leak anger reaches Parliament: opposition will demand Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan quit as the monsoon session opens tomorrow
stories.moot: Public anger becomes political power when it finds an institutional channel: a street protest can be ignored, but the same grievance raised on the floor of Parliament forces a government to respond on the record.

stories.title: A Kanpur engineering topper dies by suicide after nearly 50 failed tries at a government job — a note that reads 'Sorry, Papa'
stories.moot: When one narrow path — here, a government job — is treated as the only measure of success, its scarcity turns ordinary setbacks into crushing personal failure; broadening what counts as success is itself a safeguard.

stories.title: Delhi Police declare the 'Cockroach' party's July 20 march to Parliament illegal as a hospitalised Wangchuk refuses to break his fast
stories.moot: Protests are often timed to a political calendar for maximum leverage — and how authorities police a demonstration can matter more for public sympathy than the demonstration itself.

stories.title: Kotak Mahindra Bank's profit jumps about 26% — another solid quarter for India's private banks
stories.moot: Bank earnings are an early X-ray of the whole economy — when big lenders grow loans quickly with few bad debts, it usually means households and businesses are borrowing and repaying, a sign of underlying demand.

stories.title: Government plans jail time for insulting 'Vande Mataram' — a new bill heads to Parliament as the monsoon session opens
stories.moot: Laws about national symbols are rarely just about the symbol — they test where a democracy draws the line between fostering unity and compelling belief, and how it balances patriotism against free speech and minority rights.

stories.title: PV Sindhu wins the Japan Open — a first for any Indian, and her biggest title since 2019
stories.moot: Elite form is rarely gone for good — a champion can go two years without a title and still deliver a statement win, which is why writing off experienced players too early is a mistake.

stories.title: India's Gen Z 'cockroach' movement calls a march on Parliament — a youth revolt over exam leaks builds as the monsoon session opens
stories.moot: Movements gain their loudest moment by timing — a protest staged on the exact day power reconvenes forces the issue onto the official agenda in a way a march on any ordinary day could not.

democratic-republic-of-congo.name: Democratic Republic of Congo
democratic-republic-of-congo.primer: Ebola is a severe, often-fatal virus spread through the bodily fluids of infected people; it causes internal bleeding and organ failure and was first identified near the DRC's Ebola River in 1976. There are several species of the virus. Two approved treatments exist — but only for the Zaire species. The current outbreak is the Bundibugyo species, which has sickened people since 2007 and against which no drug has ever been formally tested. Running such a trial is hard because outbreaks are remote, fast-moving and dangerous.

stories.title: First-ever treatment trial for this Ebola strain begins at record speed
stories.moot: Having a drug is not the same as knowing it works; without controlled trials, medicine is guesswork — so the ability to run a rigorous study fast, in the middle of a crisis, is itself a life-saving capability.

stories.title: DR Congo Ebola outbreak remains one of the deadliest in years
stories.moot: Large outbreaks strain fragile health systems and carry cross-border risk.

stories.title: Ebola outbreak in DR Congo passes 300 deaths
stories.moot: Beyond the human tragedy, large Ebola outbreaks strain fragile health systems and carry the risk — however contained so far — of spreading across borders.

africa.name: Africa
africa.primer: Ebola is a severe, often-fatal viral disease — high fever, and in bad cases internal bleeding — spread through contact with an infected person's bodily fluids. The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), in central Africa, has battled repeated outbreaks. This one was declared on May 15, 2026, in the country's east, a region where armed rebel groups operate, which makes health work dangerous.

stories.title: Congo's Ebola outbreak becomes the fastest-growing on record — at least 930 dead, spreading faster than trackers can follow
stories.moot: A disease outbreak is a race between how fast a virus spreads and how fast responders can find and isolate cases — when insecurity, distrust, or unpaid staff slow the response, even a well-known disease can outrun containment.

stories.title: The fastest-spreading Ebola outbreak ever is raging in DR Congo — 2,000+ cases and 754 dead, and there's no vaccine that works on this strain
stories.moot: A medical breakthrough only protects you against the exact problem it was built for. The world has powerful Ebola vaccines — but they target one strain, so a different strain can leave responders back at square one. Preparedness isn't a single tool; it's having tools for each version of a threat, and a fast, trusted public-health system to use them.

stories.title: First-ever treatment trial for this Ebola strain begins at record speed
stories.moot: Having a drug is not the same as knowing it works; without controlled trials, medicine is guesswork — so the ability to run a rigorous study fast, in the middle of a crisis, is itself a life-saving capability.

stories.title: DR Congo Ebola outbreak remains one of the deadliest in years
stories.moot: Large outbreaks strain fragile health systems and carry cross-border risk.

stories.title: Ebola outbreak in DR Congo passes 300 deaths
stories.moot: Beyond the human tragedy, large Ebola outbreaks strain fragile health systems and carry the risk — however contained so far — of spreading across borders.

south-korea.name: South Korea
south-korea.primer: SK Hynix is a South Korean maker of memory chips — the components that store data and feed the graphics processors (like Nvidia's) that train AI. As AI demand exploded, so did demand for the high-bandwidth memory Hynix specialises in. It listed shares in the US to tap the world's deepest investor pool and fund new factories. Meanwhile, after two years of an AI-led bull market, some investors are starting to ask whether the theme is overheating.

stories.title: AI's reckoning gets real — 200+ experts demand action on its economic impact, the White House scrambles on power costs, and SK Hynix crashes 15%
stories.moot: A booming industry and an overheating trade are different things — real money can keep pouring into a technology's build-out even as the stocks that led it wobble and its hidden costs (power, jobs) come due. Watch where capital rotates next, not just the headline demand.

stories.title: SK Hynix's record chip debut soars 13% — even as Wall Street starts whispering 'AI fatigue'
stories.moot: A booming industry and an overheating trade are different things — record money can pour into a sector's real build-out even as the stocks that led it start to tire, so watch where capital rotates next, not just the headline demand.

stories.title: SK Hynix raises $26.5bn in the biggest-ever foreign debut on the Nasdaq
stories.moot: Watch where the big money actually goes: a record capital raise into one industry is a concrete, high-conviction signal about where investors think demand is heading — louder than any forecast.

stories.title: NVIDIA and SK hynix deepen the AI-memory alliance
stories.moot: Modern AI runs on a tight stack of GPUs plus ultra-fast memory.

japan.name: Japan
japan.primer: For years Japan treated crypto mainly as a 'means of payment' under its Payment Services Act — a law written to police money transfers, not investments. That left Japanese crypto traders facing some of the world's harshest taxes (gains treated as 'miscellaneous income' at rates up to 55%) and gave regulators no clean legal route to approve mainstream investment products. Japan was one of the earliest crypto hubs and the scene of huge exchange hacks (Mt. Gox, Coincheck), so it has long wrestled with how to supervise the asset.

stories.title: PV Sindhu wins the Japan Open — a first for any Indian, and her biggest title since 2019
stories.moot: Elite form is rarely gone for good — a champion can go two years without a title and still deliver a statement win, which is why writing off experienced players too early is a mistake.

stories.title: Japan just moved crypto into the same rulebook as stocks and bonds — clearing the path for Bitcoin ETFs and a much lower tax
stories.moot: How an asset is legally classified shapes everything around it — the tax you pay, who is allowed to sell it to you, and what products can be built on top. Moving crypto from a 'payments' law to an 'investments' law is what turns a fringe token into something a pension fund or a cautious saver can actually hold.

stories.title: India signs a trade deal with New Zealand and hardens its terror line with Japan
stories.moot: When the biggest trading power turns protectionist, mid-sized economies hedge by signing more deals with each other — trade diversification is a country's insurance policy against a single partner's mood swings.

stories.title: Japan's PM visits India to deepen an Indo-Pacific partnership
stories.moot: For India, closer ties with Japan mean investment, technology and a stronger hand in Asia's balance of power.

europe.name: Europe
europe.primer: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine has ground on for more than four years, and in recent weeks both sides have escalated long-range strikes on each other's cities and infrastructure. Just yesterday Ukraine launched its deadliest strike inside Russia in over two years, torching warehouses and an oil depot; this barrage is Russia's answer.

stories.title: The EU hits AliExpress with a record €550m fine for letting unsafe and fake goods flood its site
stories.moot: Regulators increasingly treat online marketplaces as responsible for what their sellers do — 'we're just the platform' no longer avoids the blame, or the bill, when customers are put at risk.

stories.title: A wildfire north of Madrid burns 13,000 hectares and empties 16 villages as Spain braces for another heatwave
stories.moot: As the climate warms, wildfire stops being a rare disaster and becomes a recurring season — the danger isn't one big fire, it's many, year after year.

stories.title: Ryanair's profit falls by a third — the first big company to show what the Iran war is doing to real businesses
stories.moot: A war can dent a company thousands of miles away through a single channel — the price of oil; whoever hasn't locked in their costs feels it first and hardest.

stories.title: Lockheed unveils a cheaper Patriot missile — a scramble to fix air defense's 'cost problem' as drones overwhelm the world's shields
stories.moot: In modern warfare, whoever wins on cost-per-shot can win the war of attrition: spending millions to destroy something that cost thousands is a losing trade, so cheaper defenses can matter as much as more powerful ones.

stories.title: Spain win the 2026 World Cup — a single extra-time goal ends Messi's last dance
stories.moot: In tournament football, a disciplined, cohesive team that controls the whole game often beats a side built around one superstar — structure can outlast individual brilliance.

stories.title: Andy Burnham becomes UK prime minister, promising to 'rewire Britain' — the fifth PM in four years
stories.moot: In a parliamentary system, the head of government can change between elections — so who leads a party matters as much as who wins the vote, and a single leadership swap can shift a whole country's economic direction overnight.

stories.title: Ukraine's leadership is in turmoil mid-war: Zelensky sacked his star defence minister, crowds are protesting for a third day, and his top general may be next
stories.moot: Even a country united by an outside enemy can fracture over strategy — and in modern war, the fight between 'more technology' and 'more troops' is itself a decisive front. How an army chooses to fight can matter as much as how much it spends.

stories.title: Europe's new digital border system is tripling airport queues — a summer-travel headache as fingerprints replace passport stamps
stories.moot: New security or tracking systems almost always trade short-term friction for long-term control — the promised benefits arrive slowly, but the queues and costs land immediately.

stories.title: Russia hits Kyiv with one of the biggest missile barrages of the war — 40+ missiles and 120+ drones
stories.moot: In modern warfare, defence is a numbers game: attackers try to overwhelm air defences with mass salvos, so a country's safety depends as much on how many interceptors it can keep in stock as on how good each one is.

stories.title: A top German conservative quits over a surrogacy row — Jens Spahn steps down after backing a ban he then used abroad
stories.moot: In politics, the gap between what a leader legislates and how they live their own life is scrutinised hard — perceived hypocrisy can end a career faster than the underlying policy fight itself.

stories.title: A fire tears through a Norwegian town and becomes the country's largest in modern times — 100+ homes gone, hundreds evacuated
stories.moot: Climate risk shows up first where no one has planned for it: when heat and drought reach places that were historically too cold or wet to burn, ordinary house fires can turn into record disasters because the buildings, water systems and fire services were never designed for it.

stories.title: India and the EU say their long-delayed free-trade deal is nearly done — aiming to sign by year-end and switch it on in early 2027
stories.moot: Trade deals take years because the last 5% is the hardest — farm goods, carbon rules and rules of origin are where national interests clash most sharply, long after the easy parts are agreed.

stories.title: The talent-spotter who found Antonelli is leaving Mercedes for Red Bull
stories.moot: In elite sport as in business, long-term success is built as much by the scouts and developers behind the scenes as by the stars on track — which is why rivals compete to hire them, not just the talent they find.

stories.title: Germany raises its national terror-threat level to 'high' — and moves to hand its spy agencies more power
stories.moot: A 'threat level' is a government translating what its spies see into a public warning — so raising it is as much a communication and policy act as a security one. And moments of heightened fear are exactly when states seek new surveillance powers, which is why every security expansion carries a parallel question the public must weigh: how much liberty are we trading for how much safety, and who watches the watchers?

stories.title: Ukraine lands its deadliest strike inside Russia in over two years: drones set Wildberries warehouses and a Moscow-region oil depot ablaze, killing at least 8
stories.moot: In modern war, logistics and supply chains are front-line targets: a side that cannot win on the battlefield can still hurt its enemy by hitting the fuel, factories and warehouses that keep the war running. And when dual-use civilian sites are struck, the line between military target and civilian harm blurs — which is exactly what each side then fights over in the information war.

stories.title: Britain gets a new prime minister without an election: Andy Burnham is declared Labour leader and will replace Keir Starmer in Downing Street on Monday
stories.moot: In a parliamentary democracy the prime minister is simply the leader of the ruling party, not a directly elected president — so a country can change its head of government in the middle of a term, without any national election, the moment the governing party picks a new chief. And rapid leadership churn (seven prime ministers in ten years) is usually a symptom, not a cause: it reflects deeper economic strain and public frustration that no single leader has been able to fix.

stories.title: Britain nationalises British Steel — the government seizes its last major steel mill from its Chinese owner to keep the country able to make its own steel
stories.moot: Some industries are treated as strategic, not just commercial: when a country decides it must be able to make its own steel, energy or chips, it will pay to keep that capability even if the business itself loses money. The market alone doesn't protect things a nation considers essential to its security — which is why governments sometimes step in and buy them.

stories.title: The EU orders Google to crack open Android and Search — rival AI assistants get 'Hey Google'-style access and competitors get its search data
stories.moot: In digital markets, the real moat isn't a single product — it's control of the gateway (the operating system, the search box) plus the mountain of data that flows through it. That is exactly why regulators are shifting from breaking up companies to forcing them to share access and data: prying open the gateway can do more for competition than any fine.

stories.title: Zelensky fires Ukraine's popular 'drone-war' defence minister — and mass protests erupt in Kyiv as parliament installs a new prime minister
stories.moot: Even under wartime rule, a leader's power ultimately rests on public trust — and removing a popular, successful official without explaining why can spark the very instability the move was meant to avoid. Transparency isn't a courtesy in a crisis; it's what keeps authority intact when people can't simply vote you out.

stories.title: Uber makes a $14.8bn move to swallow Delivery Hero — a bid to build the world's biggest food-delivery firm outside China
stories.moot: In platform businesses, markets tend to collapse toward one or two dominant players — so after a long, loss-making land grab, the endgame is consolidation: the profitable survivor buys the rest to own the network. And big acquirers now design deals to pass antitrust review, pre-selling the overlapping pieces before regulators even ask.

stories.title: France legalises assisted dying: after 14 years of debate, the National Assembly passes a law letting terminally ill adults take lethal medication
stories.moot: Big social change often turns on procedure as much as persuasion: when a two-chamber legislature deadlocks, the rules about which house gets the 'final say' can decide an outcome the moral argument alone never settled.

stories.title: India's biggest trade deal with the West goes live: the India-UK free-trade pact kicks in — cheaper Scotch and British cars coming in, near-zero tariffs for Indian exports going out
stories.moot: Free-trade deals work by trading away protection: each side gives up some of its tariffs to win access to the other's market. The winners are exporters and the consumers of the newly cheaper goods; the real test is always whether the jobs and sales gained outweigh the home industries newly exposed to competition.

stories.title: Britain is about to swap prime ministers again: Keir Starmer resigns and Andy Burnham is set to take over on Monday — the UK's 7th leader in just over a decade
stories.moot: In a parliamentary democracy a prime minister doesn't need to lose a national election to lose power — they govern only as long as their own party's MPs back them. When the party turns, the leader falls, and the country's top job can change hands without voters casting a single ballot.

stories.title: Ann Widdecombe, a well-known British politician, is killed in a 'targeted attack' — and counter-terror police take over the case
stories.moot: Political violence is measured not just by the one life lost but by the fear it spreads — when a public figure is attacked, the real target is open debate itself, which is why states treat such acts as terrorism rather than ordinary crime.

stories.title: The company that makes the machines that make every advanced AI chip just raised its outlook — again: ASML lifts its 2026 forecast on 'extremely strong' orders
stories.moot: To judge whether a boom is real, look at the base of its supply chain, not the loudest stock. The company selling the one essential tool everyone needs — the 'picks and shovels' of the gold rush — gives a cleaner read on true demand than any single product-maker, because everyone in the industry has to go through it.

stories.title: Volkswagen warns of up to 50,000 more job cuts to survive
stories.moot: In a fast technology shift, incumbents are often crippled not by running out of money but by a cost structure built for the old world — high wages and legacy factories become an anchor when nimbler rivals redefine the product.

stories.title: Hungary's parliament votes to oust Orban's ally from the presidency
stories.moot: Winning an election is only the start of taking power; entrenched leaders leave loyalists embedded across courts, the presidency and agencies, so the real struggle is often the slow, legally messy fight to control the institutions afterward.

stories.title: Summer of extremes: wildfire hits Paris's Fontainebleau forest, 10 still missing in Spain, and warm rivers cut French nuclear output
stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can burn suburbs that never burned before, strain grids, cut power output and threaten harvests at once, because infrastructure was built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Heat and fire on two continents — wildfire reaches Paris's outskirts, a 'dangerous' US heat dome builds, and 2,700 heat deaths counted in England and Wales
stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill thousands indoors, burn suburbs, strain grids and threaten harvests at once, because societies and infrastructure were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Deadly heat spans two continents — Spain's wildfire kills 12+ as a US heat dome bakes two-thirds of the country
stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill in a wildfire, close landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids across whole continents at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Europe's heat emergency turns deadly — Spanish wildfire kills 12+, Paris shuts the Eiffel Tower early
stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same heatwave can kill in a wildfire, close national landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Deadly heat and storms: a Spanish wildfire kills 12 as Typhoon Bavi bears down on Japan
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk: extreme weather is no longer just a humanitarian story but a driver of insurance costs, farm output and even chip supply — which is why markets increasingly watch the forecast.

stories.title: NATO summit wraps: €70bn for Ukraine, and Trump lets Kyiv build its own Patriot missiles
stories.moot: Owning the factory beats owning the stockpile — a licence to build your own weapons changes a country's leverage far more than any single shipment, but manufacturing capacity takes years to stand up, so the relief lags the announcement.

stories.title: Le Pen launches her 2027 campaign — ankle monitor and all
stories.moot: In modern democracies the exact terms of a sentence — which months are suspended, whether a ban is backdated — can decide whether a frontrunner is knocked out or set free, so 'political risk' now includes judicial calendars as much as ballot boxes.

stories.title: NATO's Ankara summit pledges €70bn for Ukraine — but much of it is old money, and the US won't pay
stories.moot: Read the fine print on big numbers: a headline aid figure can be mostly old money relabelled, and who actually writes the cheque (here, Europe, not the US) tells you more about real commitment than the total does.

stories.title: French court hands Le Pen a lifeline — conviction upheld, but the ban shortened enough to run in 2027
stories.moot: In modern democracies a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — but the exact length of a sentence, down to which months are suspended or backdated, can decide whether a frontrunner is knocked out or set free. Details are destiny.

stories.title: NATO summit opens in Ankara — Trump demands the money, Ukraine begs for interceptors after its worst night
stories.moot: Alliances are tested not by their promises but by their invoices — a summit reveals how much collective defence a group will actually pay for, and on the battlefield the side that can still shoot down incoming missiles keeps its bargaining power.

stories.title: France's top court rules today on whether Marine Le Pen can run for president
stories.moot: In modern democracies a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — a single ruling can remove a frontrunner and reroute a country's politics, which is why 'political risk' includes judicial calendars.

stories.title: Europe's record heat turns deadly and unequal — wildfires force evacuations in France
stories.moot: Climate change shows up first as an infrastructure-and-inequality bill — societies built for yesterday's weather must retrofit grids and homes, and who can afford to adapt, and who can't, becomes a political fight.

stories.title: Russia answers peace talk with its heaviest strike on Kyiv — and Ukraine is running out of interceptors
stories.moot: Diplomacy tends to ratify the facts on the ground, not replace them — which is why a 'peace call' can be followed by a bigger attack. On the battlefield, the side that can still defend its cities keeps its bargaining power; run out of interceptors and you run out of leverage.

israel.name: Israel
israel.primer: Ali Khamenei ruled Iran as Supreme Leader — its highest political and religious authority — for 36 years. He was assassinated on February 28, 2026, at the very start of a short but intense US-Israel war on Iran. His son, Mojtaba Khamenei, was named successor by Iran's Assembly of Experts in March but has stayed out of public view since. A fragile calm has since returned to the region, including the vital Strait of Hormuz shipping lane.

stories.title: Hundreds of Israelis march on the Gaza border to demand Jewish resettlement — with coalition ministers backing them
stories.moot: In a coalition government, a prime minister's stated policy and his actual room to maneuver can diverge — when survival depends on hardline partners, street movements they back can drag official policy in directions the leader publicly denies wanting.

stories.title: Overshadowed by the Iran war, Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza as hospitals go dark
stories.moot: A ceasefire is a label, not a guarantee — what matters is the reality underneath it: who controls the ground and whether basic systems like power and hospitals still function; watch conditions, not just declarations.

stories.title: Nine months into a ceasefire, Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza as hospitals go dark
stories.moot: A ceasefire is a label, not a guarantee — what matters is the reality underneath it: who controls the ground and whether basic systems like power and hospitals still function; watch conditions, not just declarations.

stories.title: Iran buries its assassinated Supreme Leader in a week-long show of defiance
stories.moot: Markets care less about who leads a country than about whether the transition is orderly — predictability keeps oil and risk premiums low, while uncertainty at the top of an oil power is priced into the whole world's fuel bill.

new-zealand.name: New Zealand
new-zealand.primer: India is negotiating trade deals with several partners at once — the US, UK, EU and, now, New Zealand — as it tries to widen markets for its goods and services. A free-trade agreement (FTA) is a pact that lowers tariffs (import taxes) so two countries can trade more cheaply. With the US pushing a proposed 12.5% tariff on some Indian goods, New Delhi has taken a firmer negotiating stance, betting its fast-growing market gives it leverage to wait for better terms rather than sign quickly.

stories.title: New Zealand clinch the ODI series in the Caribbean with a nerve-shredding one-wicket win
stories.moot: On a difficult pitch, runs are worth more than they look — a 'small' total can be defendable, and the team that keeps its nerve in a tight chase, not the one with the flashier batting, usually wins.

stories.title: Trade on two fronts: an 'emboldened' India holds out on a US deal as it inks a 'historic' free-trade pact with New Zealand
stories.moot: Trade negotiation is a game of alternatives — the more partners a country can deal with, the less any single one can dictate terms. India signing an easy FTA with New Zealand while holding firm with the US shows how diversification itself becomes bargaining power.

stories.title: Modi upgrades India–New Zealand ties to a 'strategic partnership' — first Indian PM visit in 40 years
stories.moot: Diplomacy compounds like interest — a run of back-to-back deals (missiles, uranium, trade, partnerships) turns individual handshakes into a strategic web, and the best time to court neglected partners is when a rival's rise makes every friend more valuable.

stories.title: India signs a trade deal with New Zealand and hardens its terror line with Japan
stories.moot: When the biggest trading power turns protectionist, mid-sized economies hedge by signing more deals with each other — trade diversification is a country's insurance policy against a single partner's mood swings.

france.name: France
france.primer: Whether the state should let a doctor help a dying person end their life is one of the oldest, most divisive questions in medical ethics — pitting personal autonomy and mercy against fears that the vulnerable, elderly or disabled could be pushed toward death. France has argued over it for more than a decade across successive governments, with reformers pointing to neighbours who already allow it and opponents (including much of the Catholic church and some doctors' groups) warning of a slippery slope. The bill had already cleared the National Assembly in earlier readings but kept hitting resistance in the Senate, France's upper house.

stories.title: France legalises assisted dying: after 14 years of debate, the National Assembly passes a law letting terminally ill adults take lethal medication
stories.moot: Big social change often turns on procedure as much as persuasion: when a two-chamber legislature deadlocks, the rules about which house gets the 'final say' can decide an outcome the moral argument alone never settled.

stories.title: Summer of extremes: wildfire hits Paris's Fontainebleau forest, 10 still missing in Spain, and warm rivers cut French nuclear output
stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can burn suburbs that never burned before, strain grids, cut power output and threaten harvests at once, because infrastructure was built for a cooler normal.

stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill thousands indoors, burn suburbs, strain grids and threaten harvests at once, because societies and infrastructure were built for a cooler normal.

stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill in a wildfire, close landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids across whole continents at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Europe's heat emergency turns deadly — Spanish wildfire kills 12+, Paris shuts the Eiffel Tower early
stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same heatwave can kill in a wildfire, close national landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.moot: In modern democracies the exact terms of a sentence — which months are suspended, whether a ban is backdated — can decide whether a frontrunner is knocked out or set free, so 'political risk' now includes judicial calendars as much as ballot boxes.

stories.moot: In modern democracies a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — but the exact length of a sentence, down to which months are suspended or backdated, can decide whether a frontrunner is knocked out or set free. Details are destiny.
